beats.text: A multi-armed bandit is the smallest RL problem: one state, several arms, each returning a noisy reward.

visual.sub: One state, K arms, one dilemma

beats.text: There are no transitions and no credit assignment over time, so it isolates the explore-exploit trade-off cleanly.
In a contextual bandit the agent sees a context x first, then picks an arm conditioned on it.

visual.note: The full RL return collapses to one expected reward

beats.text: The episode lasts exactly one step: observe, act, get rewarded, terminate, with no next state to plan toward.
Read x as the prompt, a as the whole response, and r as how good the answer was, and RLHF is a contextual bandit.
Two things are missing: where the reward comes from, and how to optimize it over a combinatorially huge action space.
People cannot reliably score a response on an absolute scale, but they can reliably compare two of them.
The Bradley-Terry model turns comparisons into a scalar: the preference probability is the sigmoid of the reward gap.

visual.tex: P\big(y_w \succ y_l \mid x\big) = \sigma\!\big(r_\phi(x, y_w) - r_\phi(x, y_l)\big)
visual.note: Only the difference of rewards matters

beats.text: Equal rewards give exactly 0.5, a coin flip, and only the gap matters, so the scale is fixed up to a shift.
The reward model is a transformer with its token head swapped for a single scalar head reading the final hidden state.

visual.note: Logistic regression on the reward gap

beats.text: When the two answers are equally good the loss hits its maximum, log 2, roughly 0.69.

visual.label: max RM loss at equal rewards

beats.text: The classic RLHF pipeline runs three stages: supervised fine-tuning, reward-model training, then policy optimization.
Stage three optimizes the policy against the reward model using PPO, chosen because it takes small, conservative steps.

visual.sub: Small steps against a fragile reward

beats.text: PPO's clipped surrogate caps the probability ratio so one update cannot move the policy too far.

visual.note: The min makes the bound pessimistic

beats.text: With epsilon at 0.2 the effective ratio is capped at 1.2, so pushing the ratio higher buys no extra objective.
RLHF adds a per-token KL penalty against the SFT model, optimizing the reward minus a leash on drift.

visual.note: Keeps the policy where the reward model is trustworthy

beats.text: Standard PPO-RLHF keeps four large models in memory at once: policy, reference, reward model, and a critic.
It works and produced InstructGPT and ChatGPT, but it is heavy, finicky, and hard to reproduce.
The KL-regularized objective is not an open search: for a fixed reward it has a known closed-form optimal policy.
That optimum is the reference distribution reweighted by the exponentiated reward, a Gibbs distribution.

visual.note: Z(x) is intractable; it sums over all sequences

beats.text: Inverting it and substituting into Bradley-Terry, the intractable partition function cancels exactly in the difference.

visual.note: The policy is its own reward model

beats.text: No reward model is trained, no rollouts are sampled, no RL loop runs, just a forward and backward pass.
With pi over ref at 2 for the chosen and 0.5 for the rejected, the model assigns the chosen completion 0.8.
DPO is offline and cannot explore beyond its fixed data, so well-tuned PPO can still edge it on the hardest tasks.
For math and code the reward needs no human: an answer is right or wrong, tests pass or they do not.
RLVR replaces the learned, hackable reward model with a deterministic checker returning a clean signal.
GRPO attacks PPO's most expensive piece, the value network, and deletes it entirely.

visual.note: Baseline is the group mean, scale the group std

beats.text: For a group of four scoring rewards one, zero, zero, one, a correct response gets a standardized advantage of one.
Sample several answers, reward each, standardize within the group, and reasoning behavior emerges unsupervised.
Reward hacking haunts every method here: the policy optimizes the measured reward, not the intended one.
